title: Authors and Citation

code: @Manual{,   title = {inflationkit: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation},   author = {Charles Coverdale},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://charlescoverdale.github.io/inflationkit/}, }

what: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: R package inflation analysis. Decompose headline inflation component contributions, compute core measures (trimmed mean, weighted median, exclusion-based), estimate persistent inflation , fit Phillips curves, extract underlying trend noisy price data.

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
code: install.packages("inflationkit")  # Or install the development version from GitHub # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("charlescoverdale/inflationkit") library(inflationkit)  # Built-in sample data: 10 CPI components, 120 months d <- ik_sample_data("components") head(d) #>         date    item weight price_change #> 1 2015-01-01    Food   0.15     0.003218 #> 2 2015-01-01 Housing   0.25     0.004274 #> 3 2015-01-01 Transport 0.12    0.001893 #> ...  # What is driving inflation? decomp <- ik_decompose(d) plot(decomp)

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: Headline inflation noisy. Food energy prices swing month month, making hard tell whether inflation genuinely rising just reacting temporary shock. Central banks solve computing “core” measures (trimmed means, weighted medians, exclusion-based indices) studying persistence, diffusion, trend. standard tools every central bank research department, general-purpose R package . CRAN package “inflation” name (Inflation) Brazil-specific updated since 2017. inflationkit fills gap. bring CPI component data country, package handles decomposition, core measures, persistence estimation, Phillips curves, trend extraction, forecast evaluation. Functions accept column names, can plug dataset without reshaping first.

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: Decompose headline inflation weighted contribution component. tells many percentage points category (food, housing, transport, etc.) adding total.
code: d <- ik_sample_data("components") decomp <- ik_decompose(d) decomp #> -- Inflation Decomposition -- #> * Period: 2015-01-01 to 2024-12-01 #> * Number of items: 10 #> * Mean headline inflation: 0.25%  plot(decomp)  # Stacked bar chart of contributions over time

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: Compare four core inflation measures side side. trimmed mean weighted median distribution-based (let data decide exclude month). exclusion method asymmetric trim use fixed rules.
code: d <- ik_sample_data("components")  # Cleveland Fed style: symmetric 8% trim tm <- ik_core(d, method = "trimmed_mean", trim = 0.08)  # Weighted median: 50th percentile of price changes wm <- ik_core(d, method = "weighted_median")  # Exclusion: drop food and transport ex <- ik_core(d, method = "exclusion", exclude = c("Food", "Transport"))  # Dallas Fed style: asymmetric trim (24% lower, 31% upper) at <- ik_core(d, method = "asymmetric_trim")  # Compare all four on one chart comp <- ik_compare(tm, wm, ex, at,                    labels = c("Trimmed mean", "Weighted median",                               "Ex food & transport", "Asymmetric trim")) plot(comp)

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: Measure quickly inflation returns mean shock. High persistence means rate hikes take longer bring inflation .
code: d <- ik_sample_data("headline")  pers <- ik_persistence(d$inflation, method = "sum_ar") pers #> -- Inflation Persistence (Sum of AR Coefficients) -- #> * AR order: 4 (selected by BIC) #> * Persistence value: 0.72 #> * Moderate persistence (sum of AR coefficients 0.5 to 0.8)  # Half-life: how many quarters until a shock decays by half? pers_hl <- ik_persistence(d$inflation, method = "half_life") pers_hl #> -- Inflation Persistence (Half-Life) -- #> * Persistence value: 3.2 periods

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: Estimate relationship inflation unemployment. negative slope means higher unemployment associated lower inflation.
code: d <- ik_sample_data("headline")  pc <- ik_phillips(d$inflation, d$unemployment, type = "traditional", lags = 4) pc #> -- Phillips Curve (Traditional) -- #> * Slope estimate: -0.12 (p = 0.03) #> * R-squared: 0.45 #> * Observations: 76  # Robust standard errors (Newey-West HAC) pc_hac <- ik_phillips(d$inflation, d$unemployment,                       type = "traditional", lags = 4, robust_se = "HAC")  plot(pc)  # Scatter plot with fitted line

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: Separate permanent trend transitory shocks using Hodrick-Prescott filter Beveridge-Nelson decomposition.
code: d <- ik_sample_data("headline")  hp <- ik_trend(d$inflation, method = "hp") hp #> -- Trend Inflation (Hodrick-Prescott) -- #> * Lambda: 1600 #> * Mean trend: 2.8 #> * Cycle volatility (SD): 0.5  plot(hp)  # Original series with trend overlay  # Beveridge-Nelson decomposition (ARIMA-based) bn <- ik_trend(d$inflation, method = "beveridge_nelson") plot(bn)

what: Are these inflation forecasts any good?
title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: Test whether forecasts unbiased (Mincer-Zarnowitz) compare two competing forecasts (Diebold-Mariano).
code: actual <- c(2.1, 2.3, 2.5, 2.2, 2.8, 3.0, 2.7, 2.4) forecast1 <- c(2.0, 2.2, 2.3, 2.1, 2.5, 2.8, 2.6, 2.3) forecast2 <- c(1.8, 2.0, 2.6, 2.3, 2.4, 3.2, 2.5, 2.1)  # Bias test bias <- ik_forecast_eval(actual, forecast1, test = "bias") bias #> -- Forecast Evaluation (Mincer-Zarnowitz Bias Test) -- #> * p-value: 0.42 #> * Cannot reject H0: no evidence of forecast bias.  # Diebold-Mariano: is forecast1 better than forecast2? dm <- ik_forecast_eval(actual, forecast1, test = "dm", forecast2 = forecast2) dm #> -- Forecast Evaluation (Diebold-Mariano Test) -- #> * p-value: 0.08 #> * No significant difference in predictive accuracy.

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: functions take data frame CPI components. row one item one period. default column names date, item, weight, price_change: data uses different column names, every function date_col, item_col, weight_col, change_col arguments can map : functions (ik_persistence, ik_phillips, ik_trend, ik_forecast_eval) take simple numeric vectors instead data frame.
code: # Your data has columns: month, category, share, pct_change ik_decompose(my_data,              date_col = "month", item_col = "category",              weight_col = "share", change_col = "pct_change")

what: Option 1: Use the built-in sample data
title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: package includes sample datasets can try everything immediately:
code: library(inflationkit)  # Component-level data: 10 CPI items, 120 months d <- ik_sample_data("components") head(d) #>         date    item weight price_change #> 1 2015-01-01    Food   0.15     0.003218 #> 2 2015-01-01 Housing   0.25     0.004274 #> 3 2015-01-01 Transport 0.12    0.001893  # Headline data: monthly inflation + unemployment h <- ik_sample_data("headline") head(h) #>         date inflation unemployment #> 1 2015-01-01    0.0025         0.055

what: Option 2: Pull UK CPI data from the ONS
title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
code: # 1. Install the data package (one time) install.packages("ons")  # 2. Download CPI component series library(ons) cpi <- ons_cpi()   # Returns monthly CPI index by component  # 3. Compute month-on-month price changes and reshape for inflationkit library(inflationkit) # Your data frame needs: date, item, weight, price_change # See ons package documentation for component weights

what: Option 3: Pull US CPI data from FRED
title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
code: # 1. Install the data package (one time) install.packages("fred")  # 2. Get a free API key from https://fred.stlouisfed.org/docs/api/api_key.html library(fred) fred_set_key("your_api_key_here")  # 3. Download CPI sub-indices food    <- fred_series("CPIFABSL")    # Food housing <- fred_series("CPIHOSSL")    # Housing transport <- fred_series("CPITRNSL")  # Transportation  # 4. Compute month-on-month changes, assign weights, and stack into #    a data frame with columns: date, item, weight, price_change

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: functions return S3 objects print() plot() methods.

title: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation
text: methods implemented package based : Bryan, M.F. & Cecchetti, S.G. (1994). “Measuring Core Inflation.” Monetary Policy, ed. N.G. Mankiw, University Chicago Press, 195-219. (NBER Working Paper w4303) Bils, M. & Klenow, P.J. (2004). “Evidence Importance Sticky Prices.” Journal Political Economy, 112(5), 947-985. doi:10.1086/422559 Marques, C.R. (2004). “Inflation Persistence: Facts Artefacts?” ECB Working Paper . 371. Beveridge, S. & Nelson, C.R. (1981). “New Approach Decomposition Economic Time Series.” Journal Monetary Economics, 7(2), 151-174. Diebold, F.X. & Mariano, R.S. (1995). “Comparing Predictive Accuracy.” Journal Business & Economic Statistics, 13(3), 253-263.

title: Compute breakeven inflation rates — ik_breakeven
text: Calculates breakeven inflation spread nominal real (inflation-linked) bond yields. provides market-based measure inflation expectations.

title: Compute breakeven inflation rates — ik_breakeven
code: ik_breakeven(nominal_yield, real_yield, maturity = NULL)

title: Compute breakeven inflation rates — ik_breakeven
text: nominal_yield Numeric vector. Nominal bond yields. real_yield Numeric vector. Real (inflation-linked) bond yields, length nominal_yield. maturity Numeric vector NULL. Bond maturities years. provided, must length nominal_yield.

title: Compute breakeven inflation rates — ik_breakeven
text: S3 object class "ik_breakeven" elements: breakeven maturity provided, data.frame columns: maturity, nominal, real, breakeven. Otherwise, numeric vector breakeven rates. maturity maturity vector (NULL).

title: Compute breakeven inflation rates — ik_breakeven
text: Note: breakeven inflation simplified measure account inflation risk premium liquidity premium. interpreted rough proxy inflation expectations, precise measure.

title: Compute breakeven inflation rates — ik_breakeven
code: # Breakeven term structure be <- ik_breakeven(   nominal_yield = c(4.2, 4.5, 4.8, 5.0),   real_yield = c(1.8, 2.0, 2.3, 2.5),   maturity = c(2, 5, 10, 30) ) print(be) #>  #> ── Breakeven Inflation Rates ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • 2Y: 2.4% (nominal 4.2%, real 1.8%) #> • 5Y: 2.5% (nominal 4.5%, real 2%) #> • 10Y: 2.5% (nominal 4.8%, real 2.3%) #> • 30Y: 2.5% (nominal 5%, real 2.5%) plot(be)   # Simple breakeven (no maturity) be2 <- ik_breakeven(nominal_yield = 4.5, real_yield = 2.0) print(be2) #>  #> ── Breakeven Inflation Rates ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Breakeven rates: 2.5%

what: Compare multiple core inflation measures — ik_compare
title: Compare multiple core inflation measures — ik_compare
text: Takes multiple ik_core objects facilitates side--side comparison. Produces summary statistics multi-line chart measures.

title: Compare multiple core inflation measures — ik_compare
code: ik_compare(..., labels = NULL)

title: Compare multiple core inflation measures — ik_compare
text: ... One objects class "ik_core", returned ik_core(). labels Character vector NULL. Labels measure. NULL, labels derived object's method name.

title: Compare multiple core inflation measures — ik_compare
text: S3 object class "ik_comparison" elements: measures List ik_core objects. labels Character vector labels.

title: Compare multiple core inflation measures — ik_compare
code: data <- ik_sample_data("components") core_tm <- ik_core(data, method = "trimmed_mean") core_wm <- ik_core(data, method = "weighted_median") core_ex <- ik_core(data, method = "exclusion", exclude = c("Food", "Transport"))  comp <- ik_compare(core_tm, core_wm, core_ex) print(comp) #>  #> ── Core Inflation Comparison ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Summary Statistics ── #>  #> • Trimmed Mean (8%): mean = 0.27%, SD = 0.00127 #> • Weighted Median: mean = 0.27%, SD = 0.00159 #> • Exclusion (Food, Transport): mean = 0.26%, SD = 0.0014 #> • Headline: mean = 0.26% plot(comp)

title: Compute core inflation measures — ik_core
text: Estimates core (underlying) inflation using one three standard methods: trimmed mean, weighted median, exclusion-based. measures aim strip transitory price movements reveal persistent trend.

title: Compute core inflation measures — ik_core
code: ik_core(   data,   method = c("trimmed_mean", "weighted_median", "exclusion", "asymmetric_trim"),   trim = 0.08,   trim_lower = 0.24,   trim_upper = 0.31,   exclude = NULL,   date_col = "date",   item_col = "item",   change_col = "price_change",   weight_col = "weight" )

title: Compute core inflation measures — ik_core
text: data data.frame containing component-level inflation data. method Character. One "trimmed_mean", "weighted_median", "exclusion", "asymmetric_trim". trim Numeric. Fraction trim tail ("trimmed_mean" ). Default 0.08 (8% symmetric trim, following Cleveland Fed). trim_lower Numeric. Fraction trim lower tail ("asymmetric_trim" ). Default 0.24, matching Dallas Fed trimmed mean PCE. trim_upper Numeric. Fraction trim upper tail ("asymmetric_trim" ). Default 0.31, matching Dallas Fed trimmed mean PCE. exclude Character vector. Items exclude ("exclusion" ). date_col Character. Name date column. Default "date". item_col Character. Name item/component column. Default "item". change_col Character. Name price change column. Default "price_change". weight_col Character. Name weight column. Default "weight".

title: Compute core inflation measures — ik_core
text: S3 object class "ik_core" elements: core data.frame columns: date, core_inflation. method Character. method used. trim Numeric. trim fraction (applicable). exclude Character vector. Excluded items (applicable). headline data.frame columns: date, headline.

title: Compute core inflation measures — ik_core
text: Bryan, M. F. Cecchetti, S. G. (1993). "Consumer Price Index Measure Inflation." NBER Working Paper . 4505. Bryan, M. F. Cecchetti, S. G. (1994). "Measuring Core Inflation." Monetary Policy, University Chicago Press.

title: Compute core inflation measures — ik_core
code: data <- ik_sample_data("components")  # Trimmed mean (default) core_tm <- ik_core(data, method = "trimmed_mean") print(core_tm) #>  #> ── Core Inflation Measure ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Method: Trimmed Mean (8%) #> • Mean core inflation: 0.27% #> • Mean headline inflation: 0.26% #> • Observations: 120  # Weighted median core_wm <- ik_core(data, method = "weighted_median") print(core_wm) #>  #> ── Core Inflation Measure ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Method: Weighted Median #> • Mean core inflation: 0.27% #> • Mean headline inflation: 0.26% #> • Observations: 120  # Exclusion-based core_ex <- ik_core(data, method = "exclusion", exclude = c("Food", "Transport")) print(core_ex) #>  #> ── Core Inflation Measure ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Method: Exclusion (Food, Transport) #> • Mean core inflation: 0.26% #> • Mean headline inflation: 0.26% #> • Observations: 120

what: Decompose inflation into weighted component contributions — ik_decompose
title: Decompose inflation into weighted component contributions — ik_decompose
text: Computes weighted contribution CPI component headline inflation. contribution item weight_i * price_change_i, headline inflation sum contributions period.

title: Decompose inflation into weighted component contributions — ik_decompose
code: ik_decompose(   data,   date_col = "date",   item_col = "item",   change_col = "price_change",   weight_col = "weight" )

title: Decompose inflation into weighted component contributions — ik_decompose
text: data data.frame containing component-level inflation data. date_col Character. Name date column. Default "date". item_col Character. Name item/component column. Default "item". change_col Character. Name price change column. Default "price_change". weight_col Character. Name weight column. Default "weight".

title: Decompose inflation into weighted component contributions — ik_decompose
text: S3 object class "ik_decomposition" elements: contributions data.frame columns: date, item, weight, price_change, contribution. headline data.frame columns: date, headline_inflation.

title: Decompose inflation into weighted component contributions — ik_decompose
code: data <- ik_sample_data("components") decomp <- ik_decompose(data) print(decomp) #>  #> ── Inflation Decomposition ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Period: 2015-01-01 to 2024-12-01 #> • Number of items: 10 #> • Mean headline inflation: 0.26% plot(decomp)

what: Compute an inflation diffusion index — ik_diffusion
title: Compute an inflation diffusion index — ik_diffusion
text: Measures breadth price increases across CPI components. period, computes (weighted unweighted) fraction items price changes exceeding threshold. diffusion index 0.5 indicates half items experiencing -threshold price increases.

title: Compute an inflation diffusion index — ik_diffusion
code: ik_diffusion(   data,   threshold = c("zero", "mean", "target"),   target = 0.02,   weighted = TRUE,   date_col = "date",   item_col = "item",   change_col = "price_change",   weight_col = "weight" )

title: Compute an inflation diffusion index — ik_diffusion
text: data data.frame containing component-level inflation data. threshold Character. threshold rule: "zero" (price_change > 0), "mean" (cross-sectional mean), "target" (annualised target rate). target Numeric. Annualised inflation target ("target" threshold ). Default 0.02 (2%). weighted Logical. TRUE (default), compute weighted fraction. FALSE, compute simple (unweighted) fraction. date_col Character. Name date column. Default "date". item_col Character. Name item/component column. Default "item". change_col Character. Name price change column. Default "price_change". weight_col Character. Name weight column. Default "weight".

title: Compute an inflation diffusion index — ik_diffusion
text: S3 object class "ik_diffusion" elements: diffusion data.frame columns: date, diffusion_index. threshold Character. threshold type used. weighted Logical. Whether weights used.

title: Compute an inflation diffusion index — ik_diffusion
code: data <- ik_sample_data("components")  # Fraction of items with rising prices d <- ik_diffusion(data, threshold = "zero") print(d) #>  #> ── Inflation Diffusion Index ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Threshold: Price change > 0 #> • Weighted: TRUE #> • Mean diffusion: 0.765 #> • Latest diffusion: 0.85 #> • Observations: 120 plot(d)

title: Evaluate inflation forecasts — ik_forecast_eval
text: Runs standard forecast evaluation tests. bias test (Mincer-Zarnowitz) checks whether forecasts unbiased. efficiency test (Nordhaus) checks whether forecast errors autocorrelated. Diebold-Mariano test compares predictive accuracy two competing forecasts.

title: Evaluate inflation forecasts — ik_forecast_eval
code: ik_forecast_eval(   actual,   forecast,   test = c("bias", "efficiency", "dm"),   forecast2 = NULL,   horizon = 1L,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater") )

title: Evaluate inflation forecasts — ik_forecast_eval
text: actual Numeric vector. Realised inflation values. forecast Numeric vector. Forecast values, length actual. test Character. One "bias", "efficiency", "dm". forecast2 Numeric vector NULL. Second forecast series (required "dm" test), length actual. horizon Integer. Forecast horizon (used Newey-West bandwidth DM test). Default 1. alternative Character. Alternative hypothesis DM test: "two.sided", "less", "greater".

title: Evaluate inflation forecasts — ik_forecast_eval
text: S3 object class "ik_forecast_eval" elements: test Character. test performed. statistic Numeric. test statistic. p_value Numeric. p-value. coefficients Named numeric vector (bias efficiency tests). conclusion Character. plain-language summary result.

title: Evaluate inflation forecasts — ik_forecast_eval
text: Diebold, F. X. Mariano, R. S. (1995). "Comparing Predictive Accuracy." Journal Business Economic Statistics, 13(3), 253-263.

title: Evaluate inflation forecasts — ik_forecast_eval
code: set.seed(42) data <- ik_sample_data("headline") actual <- data$inflation[5:80] forecast1 <- data$inflation[4:79] + rnorm(76, 0, 0.2) forecast2 <- data$inflation[4:79] + rnorm(76, 0, 0.4)  # Mincer-Zarnowitz bias test bias <- ik_forecast_eval(actual, forecast1, test = "bias") print(bias) #>  #> ── Forecast Evaluation ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Test: Mincer-Zarnowitz Bias Test #> • Test statistic: 19.5379 #> • p-value: 0 #> • Reject H0: forecasts appear biased (alpha != 0 or beta != 1). #> • Coefficients: alpha = 0.7846, beta = 0.4673  # Diebold-Mariano test dm <- ik_forecast_eval(actual, forecast1, test = "dm", forecast2 = forecast2) print(dm) #>  #> ── Forecast Evaluation ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Test: Diebold-Mariano Test #> • Test statistic: -3.3516 #> • p-value: 8e-04 #> • Forecast 1 is significantly more accurate than Forecast 2. #> • Coefficients: mean_loss_diff = -0.126

title: Measure inflation persistence — ik_persistence
text: Estimates degree persistence inflation series using one three methods: sum AR coefficients, half-life, largest autoregressive root.

title: Measure inflation persistence — ik_persistence
code: ik_persistence(   x,   method = c("sum_ar", "half_life", "largest_root"),   ar_order = NULL,   max_order = 12L,   ic = c("bic", "aic") )

title: Measure inflation persistence — ik_persistence
text: x Numeric vector. inflation time series. method Character. One "sum_ar", "half_life", "largest_root". ar_order Integer NULL. AR order fit. NULL, order selected automatically using information criterion specified ic. max_order Integer. Maximum AR order consider selecting automatically. Default 12. ic Character. Information criterion order selection: "bic" "aic". Default "bic".

title: Measure inflation persistence — ik_persistence
text: S3 object class "ik_persistence" elements: value Numeric. persistence measure. method Character. method used. ar_order Integer. AR order fitted. ar_coefficients Numeric vector. estimated AR coefficients. interpretation Character. plain-language interpretation ("High persistence", "Moderate persistence", "Low persistence").

title: Measure inflation persistence — ik_persistence
text: Andrews, D. W. K. Chen, H.-Y. (1994). "Approximately Median-Unbiased Estimation Autoregressive Models." Journal Business Economic Statistics, 12(2), 187-204. Marques, C. R. (2004). "Inflation Persistence: Facts Artefacts?" ECB Working Paper . 371.

title: Measure inflation persistence — ik_persistence
code: data <- ik_sample_data("headline") p <- ik_persistence(data$inflation, method = "sum_ar") print(p) #>  #> ── Inflation Persistence ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Method: Sum of AR Coefficients #> • AR order: 1 #> • Persistence value: 0.647 #> • Moderate persistence (sum of AR coefficients 0.5 to 0.8)  p_hl <- ik_persistence(data$inflation, method = "half_life") print(p_hl) #>  #> ── Inflation Persistence ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Method: Half-Life #> • AR order: 1 #> • Persistence value: 1.592 #> • Low persistence (half-life < 4 periods)

title: Estimate a Phillips curve — ik_phillips
text: Fits Phillips curve relating inflation economic slack measure (output gap unemployment rate). Supports traditional, expectations- augmented, hybrid specifications.

title: Estimate a Phillips curve — ik_phillips
code: ik_phillips(   inflation,   slack,   expectations = NULL,   type = c("traditional", "expectations_augmented", "hybrid"),   lags = 4L,   robust_se = FALSE )

title: Estimate a Phillips curve — ik_phillips
text: inflation Numeric vector. Inflation rate series. slack Numeric vector. Slack measure (output gap unemployment rate), length inflation. expectations Numeric vector NULL. Inflation expectations series, required "expectations_augmented" "hybrid" types. Must length inflation. type Character. Phillips curve specification: "traditional", "expectations_augmented", "hybrid". lags Integer. Number lagged inflation terms include. Default 4. robust_se Logical character. FALSE, use OLS standard errors. TRUE "HC1", compute HC1 heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors. "HAC", compute Newey-West heteroskedasticity autocorrelation consistent standard errors automatic bandwidth selection (Newey West, 1994). Default FALSE.

title: Estimate a Phillips curve — ik_phillips
text: S3 object class "ik_phillips" elements: coefficients Named numeric vector estimated coefficients. std_errors Named numeric vector standard errors. p_values Named numeric vector p-values. r_squared Numeric. R-squared regression. type Character. Phillips curve type. slope_estimate Numeric. estimated slope slack variable. n_obs Integer. Number observations used. residuals Numeric vector. Regression residuals.

title: Estimate a Phillips curve — ik_phillips
code: data <- ik_sample_data("headline") pc <- ik_phillips(data$inflation, data$unemployment, type = "traditional") print(pc) #>  #> ── Phillips Curve Estimation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Type: Traditional #> • Slope estimate: -0.1475 (p = 0.0801) * #> • R-squared: 0.4342 #> • Observations: 76 plot(pc)

title: Generate sample inflation data — ik_sample_data
text: Creates synthetic data testing demonstrating inflationkit functions. Two types available: component-level CPI data headline macro data.

title: Generate sample inflation data — ik_sample_data
code: ik_sample_data(type = c("components", "headline"))

title: Generate sample inflation data — ik_sample_data
text: type Character. Either "components" item-level CPI data weights price changes, "headline" quarterly macro data inflation, output gap, unemployment.

title: Generate sample inflation data — ik_sample_data
text: data.frame. "components": columns date, item, weight, price_change (120 months, 10 items, 1200 rows). "headline": columns date, inflation, output_gap, unemployment (80 quarterly observations).

what: Decompose inflation into sticky and flexible components — ik_sticky_flexible
title: Decompose inflation into sticky and flexible components — ik_sticky_flexible
text: Splits CPI components sticky-price flexible-price categories based user-provided classification, computes separate weighted inflation measures group. follows Atlanta Fed methodology.

title: Decompose inflation into sticky and flexible components — ik_sticky_flexible
code: ik_sticky_flexible(   data,   classification,   date_col = "date",   item_col = "item",   change_col = "price_change",   weight_col = "weight" )

title: Decompose inflation into sticky and flexible components — ik_sticky_flexible
text: data data.frame containing component-level inflation data. classification named logical vector data.frame. named logical vector, names correspond item names TRUE indicates sticky. data.frame, must columns item (character) sticky (logical). date_col Character. Name date column. Default "date". item_col Character. Name item/component column. Default "item". change_col Character. Name price change column. Default "price_change". weight_col Character. Name weight column. Default "weight".

title: Decompose inflation into sticky and flexible components — ik_sticky_flexible
text: S3 object class "ik_sticky_flex" elements: result data.frame columns: date, sticky, flexible, headline. classification Named logical vector mapping items sticky/flexible.

title: Decompose inflation into sticky and flexible components — ik_sticky_flexible
text: Bils, M. Klenow, P. J. (2004). "Evidence Importance Sticky Prices." Journal Political Economy, 112(5), 947-985.

title: Decompose inflation into sticky and flexible components — ik_sticky_flexible
code: data <- ik_sample_data("components") # Classify items class_vec <- c(   Food = FALSE, Housing = TRUE, Transport = FALSE,   Clothing = FALSE, Health = TRUE, Education = TRUE,   Communication = TRUE, Recreation = FALSE,   Restaurants = TRUE, Other = FALSE ) sf <- ik_sticky_flexible(data, classification = class_vec) print(sf) #>  #> ── Sticky vs Flexible Price Inflation ────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Sticky items: 5 #> • Flexible items: 5 #> • Mean sticky inflation: 0.3% #> • Mean flexible inflation: 0.23% #> • Correlation: -0.042 #> • Observations: 120 plot(sf)

title: Estimate trend inflation — ik_trend
text: Extracts trend component inflation series using one four methods: Hodrick-Prescott filter, Beveridge-Nelson decomposition, exponential smoothing, centred moving average.

title: Estimate trend inflation — ik_trend
code: ik_trend(   x,   method = c("hp", "beveridge_nelson", "exponential_smooth", "moving_average"),   frequency = c("quarterly", "monthly", "annual"),   lambda = NULL,   window = NULL )

title: Estimate trend inflation — ik_trend
text: x Numeric vector. inflation time series. method Character. One "hp", "beveridge_nelson", "exponential_smooth", "moving_average". frequency Character. Data frequency: "quarterly" (default), "monthly", "annual". Used set HP filter lambda (lambda = NULL) moving average window (window = NULL). lambda Numeric NULL. Smoothing parameter HP filter. NULL, defaults based frequency: 6.25 annual, 1600 quarterly (Hodrick Prescott, 1997), 14400 monthly (Backus Kehoe, 1992). window Integer NULL. Window size moving average method. Defaults 4 quarterly, 12 monthly, 3 annual.

title: Estimate trend inflation — ik_trend
text: S3 object class "ik_trend" elements: trend Numeric vector. estimated trend component. cycle Numeric vector. cyclical component (original minus trend). method Character. method used. lambda Numeric. HP filter lambda (applicable). window Integer. Moving average window (applicable). alpha Numeric. Exponential smoothing parameter (applicable). original Numeric vector. original series.

title: Estimate trend inflation — ik_trend
text: Hodrick, R. J. Prescott, E. C. (1997). "Postwar U.S. Business Cycles: Empirical Investigation." Journal Money, Credit Banking, 29(1), 1-16. Ravn, M. O. Uhlig, H. (2002). "Adjusting Hodrick-Prescott Filter Frequency Observations." Review Economics Statistics, 84(2), 371-376.

title: Estimate trend inflation — ik_trend
code: data <- ik_sample_data("headline") tr <- ik_trend(data$inflation, method = "hp") print(tr) #>  #> ── Trend Inflation ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Method: Hodrick-Prescott (lambda = 1600) #> • Mean trend: 1.5261 #> • Cycle volatility (SD): 0.3405 #> • Observations: 80 plot(tr)   tr_ma <- ik_trend(data$inflation, method = "moving_average", window = 4) print(tr_ma) #>  #> ── Trend Inflation ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> • Method: Moving Average (window = 4) #> • Mean trend: 1.5298 #> • Cycle volatility (SD): 0.2118 #> • Observations: 80

what: inflationkit: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation — inflationkit-package
title: inflationkit: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation — inflationkit-package
text: Tools analysing inflation dynamics. Computes weighted contributions price index components, core inflation measures (trimmed mean, weighted median, exclusion-based) following Bryan Cecchetti (1994), inflation persistence via sum--AR-coefficients, diffusion indices, Phillips curve estimation, breakeven inflation, trend inflation using Beveridge-Nelson decomposition Hodrick-Prescott filter. functions pure computation work price data source.

title: inflationkit: Inflation Decomposition, Core Measures, and Trend Estimation — inflationkit-package
text: Maintainer: Charles Coverdale charlesfcoverdale@gmail.com

title: inflationkit 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-03-26 Initial release. CPI component decomposition weighted contributions via ik_decompose(). Core inflation measures (trimmed mean, weighted median, exclusion-based, asymmetric trim) via ik_core(), following Bryan Cecchetti (1994). Sticky vs flexible price decomposition via ik_sticky_flexible(), based Atlanta Fed methodology (Bils Klenow, 2004). Inflation persistence estimation (sum AR coefficients, half-life via companion matrix, largest root) via ik_persistence(). Diffusion indices via ik_diffusion(). Phillips curve estimation (traditional, expectations-augmented, hybrid) via ik_phillips(). Newey-West HAC standard errors via robust_se = "HAC". Breakeven inflation nominal real yields via ik_breakeven(). Trend inflation extraction (Hodrick-Prescott filter frequency-based lambda, Beveridge-Nelson decomposition, exponential smoothing, moving average) via ik_trend(). Forecast evaluation (Mincer-Zarnowitz bias, Nordhaus efficiency, Diebold-Mariano test) via ik_forecast_eval(). Side--side comparison core inflation measures via ik_compare(). Built-sample data via ik_sample_data().
